pattern: Greedy denomination dispensing from largest to smallest is correct here because every banknote value divides all larger ones, so committing the most large notes first never blocks an otherwise-possible withdrawal.
hints: Keep a running count of how many notes you hold of each of the five fixed denominations, updating it on every deposit.
For a withdrawal, walk the denominations from largest to smallest and take as many of each as both the request and your stock allow.
Compute the full plan first without touching your stock; only if the remaining amount reaches exactly zero do you subtract the plan and return it, otherwise return [-1] and leave the stock unchanged.

edge_inputs.description: statement example: a successful withdraw, a rejected greedy-trap withdraw, then another success
greedy commits the largest note and then cannot finish, so the request is rejected even though a non-greedy split exists
withdraw of zero-stock machine is rejected; deposit then exact-drain works
amount not reachable by any combination of denominations on hand
a rejected withdraw must not consume notes, so the following withdraw still succeeds
large stock and a large multi-note withdrawal

stress.description: interleaved deposits and withdrawals; withdrawal amounts are multiples of 20 so many succeed and the greedy path is exercised both ways
stress.note: amounts are multiples of 20 (the smallest note) so dispensability depends on stock, not divisibility alone
